component_identification.content: Standard Coup set contains 15 influence cards: 3 Duke, 3 Assassin, 3 Captain, 3 Ambassador, 3 Contessa; plus coin tokens (commonly 50) and quick reference cards. Each player begins with 2 coins and 2 hidden influence cards. The shared influence deck remains face down and is public in composition but private in identity. For setup checks, verify exactly three copies of each role exist—probability and bluff logic depend on that fixed distribution.

core_game_loop.content: On your turn, declare one action (income, foreign aid, tax, steal, exchange, assassinate, or coup when affordable). Other players may challenge the truth of claimed role actions, and certain actions can also be blocked by specific claimed roles. Resolve challenge/block windows in order, then apply action effect if not canceled. Players who lose influence reveal one card and continue with remaining hidden card if any. Last player with influence remaining wins.

detailed_rules.content: Deal two influence cards face down to each player; place remaining role cards as court deck; give each player two coins. Legal actions: Income (+1, unchallengeable), Foreign Aid (+2, blockable by Duke), Coup (pay 7, target loses one influence, cannot be blocked), Tax as Duke (+3), Assassinate as Assassin (pay 3, target loses one influence, blockable by Contessa), Steal as Captain (take up to 2 from target, blockable by Captain or Ambassador), Exchange as Ambassador (draw two from deck, choose two to keep, return rest). Any role claim may be challenged. If claimant proves claim by revealing correct role card, challenger loses one influence and claimant may swap revealed card with random deck card to restore secrecy. If claimant fails challenge, claimant loses one influence and claimed action/block fails. Blocks may themselves be challenged similarly. Turn economy pressure rule: if a player starts turn with 10+ coins, they must coup. Eliminated players (no influence left) are out immediately and cannot challenge or block. Hidden information is the heart of play; avoid accidental card exposure during exchanges. Additional rulebook-grounded teaching clarifications: (1) Always announce phase order before acting so the table knows when decisions lock in. (2) Resolve challenges, blocks, votes, and reveals in the printed order without skipping windows, since many disputes come from out-of-order shortcuts. (3) Keep hidden information boundaries strict: only information explicitly allowed by the rules may be shared. (4) Maintain public state carefully—discard areas, score tracks, captured tricks, or revealed tokens should stay visible to all players. (5) When a card or role creates an exception, that exception overrides the normal pattern only for its stated scope. (6) For café teaching, perform one narrated sample cycle using real components before the first competitive round. (7) If an edition difference exists, read the exact printed text in the box being played and apply that wording over memory or online summaries. (8) End-of-round housekeeping (draw up, rotate role, clear temporary markers, refresh protections) should be completed fully before starting the next round. (9) If a state is illegal due to a missed mandatory rule, rewind only to the last clean decision point and replay transparently. (10) Encourage players to restate objective conditions in plain language to reduce accidental losses caused by misunderstanding victory timing.

scoring_and_endgame.content: Coup has elimination victory, not points. You “score” only by survival: each lost influence card reduces your life total from 2 to 1 to 0. Coup and assassination effects force influence loss; failed challenges also force influence loss. Game ends instantly when only one player has at least one unrevealed influence card remaining. No tie condition in standard rules because elimination order is sequential.

beginner_strategy.content: Early game, prioritize stable income lines and avoid unnecessary high-variance challenges. Challenge only when probability and table context justify it (e.g., too many of a role already revealed). Track revealed cards constantly; once two Contessas are out, assassination threats change dramatically. Keep coin pressure in mind—forcing an opponent to coup can make their turn predictable. Mix truthful and bluff claims so opponents cannot map your behavior one-to-one with card reality. Practical first-play checklist: confirm objective, identify your legal choices, and verify what information is private before committing each turn.

metadata.notes: Rewrite sprint using official rulebook text from coup.txt as primary source. Reference snippet: COMPONENTS: 15 character cards (3 each of 5 characters), 50 coins, summary cards.
